1 
 
H-Bridge DC motor controller Mini-Project 
19201418 
October 2025 
Introduction 
This report will analyse circuitry of an H-bridge DC motor controller. The report will 
outline the key theory necessary to understand the operation of an H-bridge 
controller. Then demonstrate efficient circuit design through modifications and 
additions, working towards a real-world application: an elevator motor. In accordance 
with the report’s objectives below, evidence will be provided to justify design 
decisions. 
Objectives: 
- To redesign a simple H-bridge DC motor controller in order to control motor 
speed and acceleration for use as elevator drive, whilst maintaining simple 
and practical circuits. 
- To construct and simulate the circuits using Multisim software to verify and 
discuss performance. 
- To consider the practical limitations of the circuits and their components: 
suggesting mitigation strategies and assessing other real-world factors such 
as sustainability and ethics. 
The

the circuits and their components: 
suggesting mitigation strategies and assessing other real-world factors such 
as sustainability and ethics. 
The final section of the report will also detail design and testing of a hardware 
prototype circuit, further demonstrating the understanding and considerations 
required for successful circuit implementation. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Part A 
i)  
The circuit in ‘Fig. 1’ provides a simple basis to understand H-bridge circuit 
operation. A 12V power source delivers current to the load – two LEDs with opposing 
polarity. The current is routed by the four mechanical switches in a ‘H’ shape 
Fig. 1: Basic H-bridge circuit powering opposed LEDs

2 
 
configuration. The state of the switches determines the polarity of current through the 
load. Diagonal pairs allow current to flow.[1] Note that two switches on the same ‘leg’ 
(left leg: S1 and S3, right leg: S2 and S4) should never be closed at the same time 
as this would short the circuit to ground – a ‘shoot-through’ error. 
For the first modification in ‘Fig. 2’ the LEDs are replaced by a DC motor ‘M1’. When 
the switches allow forward bias the motor is powered clockwise, and when reverse 
biased - anticlockwise. 
 
 
 
The use of four mechanical switches is impractical. To improve automation, the 
switches are replaced by four Bipolar Junction Transistors (BJTs) and a digital 
switching system is implemented. 
The four transistors function as switches. Q1 and Q2 are PNP transistors, activated 
by a low signal to the base. Q3 and Q4 are NPN transistors, activated by a high 
signal to the base. Otherwise, current can not flow through collector – emitter; an 
open switch.[2]

and Q4 are NPN transistors, activated by a high 
signal to the base. Otherwise, current can not flow through collector – emitter; an 
open switch.[2] 
A system of logic gates controls the signals to the bases. The wires in ‘Fig. 2’ have 
been colour coded for visualisation. There are three desirable states: motor runs 
clockwise, motor runs anticlockwise, and motor freewheels (off). In ‘Fig.2’ there are 
two switches, A and B. The logic system uses NOT and AND gates to create two 
control signals: 
- Blue: controls transistors Q2 and Q3 for clockwise operation. 
- Green: controls transistors Q1 and Q4 for anticlockwise operation 
Fig. 2: Redesigned H-bridge circuit with digital 
switching system and bidirectional motor control

3 
 
We know the motor is driven when diagonal transistor pairs are activated, so: 
 Q1 and Q4 – driven by green signal, activated when green = 1  
 Q2 and Q3 – driven by blue signal, activated when blue = 1 
The final NOT gates U1A and U2A invert the signal into Q1 and Q2 accounting for 
the difference in high-drive NPNs and low-drive PNPs. 
The following Boolean expressions describe the two signals: 
𝐺𝑅𝐸𝐸𝑁 =  𝐴̅∙ 𝐵 
𝐵𝐿𝑈𝐸 = 𝐴 ∙ 𝐵 
Hence, the truth table, ‘Fig. 3’, details all logic states. 
A B 𝐀̅ ∙ 𝐁 𝐀 ∙ 𝐁 Q1 Q2 Q3 Q4 Operation 
0 0 0 0 OFF OFF OFF OFF Off 
0 1 1 0 ON OFF OFF ON Anticlockwise 
1 0 0 0 OFF OFF OFF OFF Off 
1 1 0 1 OFF ON ON OFF Clockwise 
 
 
‘Fig. 3’ verifies the design. Only two cases power a diagonal transistor pair: A=0 , 
B=1 (anticlockwise) and A=1 , B=1 (clockwise). Two transistors on the same leg are 
never on at once: no static shoot through. Multisim simulation oscilloscope data is 
shown in below. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
The voltage output is directly pr

at once: no static shoot through. Multisim simulation oscilloscope data is 
shown in below. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
The voltage output is directly proportional to the angular velocity of the motor, so can 
be used to analyse motor operation. Initially the motor speed increases in the 
positive direction with input 1 , 1. Switch A is changed at 150ms, so: 0 , 1 input, 
reversing direction and the motor spins in the negative direction. At 400ms switch B 
Fig. 3: Logic truth table 
Fig. 4: Multisim simulation data for motor control operation

4 
 
is also turned off (0 , 0) and the motor receives no drive. It remains at constant 
velocity because the shaft friction is negligible in this simulation. 
In practical implementation of this circuit the main limitation is ‘dynamic shoot-
through’. Although static shoot-through is prevented, the transistors take a non-zero 
amount of time to switch – specifically taking longer to turn off. This can cause a 
momentary short circuit[1], e.g. clockwise to anticlockwise, Q1 will turn on before Q3 
has turned off – shorting the circuit. This results in brief pulses of large current 
leading to potential failure of the transistors. An improved logic system would have 
in-built delay – dead time – to ensure no overlap[3]. A motor is also an ‘inductive load’. 
As it turns off, the magnetic field dissipating creates a voltage spike exceeding the 
transistor breakdown voltage, destroying them. Flyback diodes should be used to 
reroute this inductive current.[4]

transistor breakdown voltage, destroying them. Flyback diodes should be used to 
reroute this inductive current.[4]  
 
ii)  
 
‘Fig. 5’ presents a modified version of the circuit, introducing a PWM voltage supply 
circuit (pink) with the aim of controlling motor speed. The PWM (555 timer in this 
case) outputs a square wave signal (0V – 5V) that activates the new transistor Q5. 
The ‘Duty Cycle’ is the percentage of time the signal is high (5V), as given in 
equation 1. 
Fig. 5: H-bridge motor control circuit with integrated PWM oscillator

5

𝑡𝑂𝑁

Where T is the time period, and tON is time of high signal. The duty cycle controls the 
average power received by the motor (equation 2). The motor responds to the 
average power produced by the high-low oscillations of the PWM circuit.[5] 
                                              𝑉𝑎𝑣𝑔 =  𝑉𝑠𝑢𝑝𝑝𝑙𝑦  ∙ 𝐷                                      (2) 
The PWM design in ‘Fig. 5’ uses an RC circuit that determines the square wave 
properties. It was designed for a duty cycle of 55% and frequency 1000Hz following 
equations 1 to 6 and with chosen capacitance C1 = 1µF.

This circuit design works as intended and meets specified 1000Hz-1500Hz 
frequency. However, the duty cycle is fixed by the component values, to adjust motor 
speed the components would have to be physically replaced. For the desired speed 
control, the duty cycle should be variable from 0% to 100%. A potentiometer could 
replace R6 to adjust the RC timing circuit, varying the duty cycle. The issue then is 
that the frequency is no longer constant. In addition, as shown below, the duty cycle 
can only be varied from 50% to 100%: no low-speed control. 
𝐷 =  𝑡𝑂𝑁
𝑇  
𝐷 =  0.693 ∙ 𝐶(𝑅1 + 𝑅2)
0.693 ∙ 𝐶(𝑅1 + 2𝑅2)

(𝑅1 + 𝑅2)
(𝑅1 + 2𝑅2) 
So even as R1 approaches 0Ω the duty cycle becomes: 
𝐷 =  
(0 + 𝑅2)
(0 + 2𝑅2) =  50%

6 
 
The final circuit design ‘Fig. 6’ corrects this issue, allowing for full motor speed 
control and a small frequency variation inside the required range. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Two 1N4148 diodes are utilised to separate the charging and discharging paths: 
- Charge path (tON): current directed from VCC, through R1, into the 
potentiometer for charging. 
- Discharge path (tOFF): discharge current from C1 directed to the DIS pin of the 
timer through the opposite end of the potentiometer. 
This allows for nearly ideal 1% to 99% duty cycle control: full adjustment of motor 
speed. The component value selection is verified by equation 3: 
𝑓 =  1.44
15𝑛𝐹(100𝑘Ω + 2 ∙ 1𝑘Ω ) = 941.176. . 𝐻𝑧 
Fig. 6: Improved PWM oscillator design 
Fig. 7: Output data at 99% duty cycle Fig. 8: Output data at 1% duty cycle

7 
 
‘Fig. 7’ and ‘Fig. 8’ show the motor output at the extremes of the potentiometer, 99% 
duty cycle and 1% duty cycle, respectively. Once motor speed stabilises at its 
maximum, the full range of speed control is observed: 220mV to 33.7V. The actual 
frequency is lower than the theoretical expectation, varying between 530Hz and 
620Hz. Likely due to the real-world characteristics of the diodes and 555 timer. 
Note: motor shaft friction increased to 1e-4 Nms/rad otherwise the simulation ran too 
long, and motor accelerated indefinitely. 
 
iii)  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
‘Fig. 9’ suggests how the circuit could monitor and account for load changes. This is 
known as a ‘closed-loop speed control system’[6]. The aim is to maintain the ‘actual 
motor speed’ close to the chosen input speed - set by Vi. A speed sensor 
(tachometer) measures actual motor speed, and then a frequency – voltage 
converter provides a ‘feedback voltage’ signal Vfb. The summing junction (e.g. a 
‘differe

easures actual motor speed, and then a frequency – voltage 
converter provides a ‘feedback voltage’ signal Vfb. The summing junction (e.g. a 
‘differential amplifier’[7]) receives both Vi and Vfb calculating the difference: Verror. This 
feeds into the 555 PWM controller, adjusting the duty cycle to account for the error.[8] 
The average power supplied to the H-bridge and thus motor speed change, creating 
a continuous feedback loop, compensating for load changes. 
Fig. 9: Block diagram for closed loop speed control system

8 
 
Part B  
 
For practical use as an elevator motor the PWM timer circuit has been further 
modified in ‘Fig. 10’. The motor must gradually accelerate / decelerate for passenger 
comfort. A ‘Voltage Ramp Generator’ circuit (red) generates a smooth DC ramp[9][10]: 
rising or falling over approximately 10 seconds. This feeds the control (CON) pin of 
the 555 timer, in turn ramping the duty cycle between 0% and 100%.  
The PWM oscillator has been reverted to the fixed 1kHz frequency circuit for 
simplicity. The ramp generator is constructed with a 10 second RC timer. Component 
values are justified as follows: 
 A capacitor fully (~99.3%) charges / discharges in 5τ (time constants) 
 Thus: required time constant: 5τ = 10s , τ = 2s 
 A large standard capacitor with capacitance = 100µF was chosen, so 
resistance is found: 
                                         𝑅 =  
τ
𝐶                                                      (7) 
𝑅 =  2
100𝜇 = 20𝑘Ω 
The SPDT switch S1 selects either t

𝑅 =  
τ
𝐶                                                      (7) 
𝑅 =  2
100𝜇 = 20𝑘Ω 
The SPDT switch S1 selects either the charging – ‘ramp up’ path or the discharging - 
‘ramp down' path through the R8 and R9 20kΩ resistors, respectively. This ensures a 
symmetrical 10 second ramp in either direction. So, for elevator operation: S1 is set 
Fig. 10: PWM oscillator modified for voltage ramp up/down control

9 
 
to ‘charge’ position, and the elevator accelerates. As the elevator approaches its 
destination S1 is switched to the ‘discharge’ position, causing deceleration to a stop. 
Finally, the Op-amp U3 is incorporated as a ‘voltage follower’. It has a very high input 
impedance, so the small current that the CON pin draws cannot distort the 10 
second timings, cleanly isolating the timing circuit from the PWM oscillator circuit.[11] 
Voltage Ramp Up: 
 
‘Fig. 11’ shows the voltage ramp up over approximately 0.45s. Simulating in Multisim 
for much longer than this is not feasible due to hardware / time constraints. The 
voltage input at the CON pin is shown to increase by 447mV in this time.  
Voltage Ramp Down: 
 
 
 
‘Fig. 12’ shows the voltage ramp down, also over approximately 0.45s This time the 
voltage decreases at a greater rate, falling by 900mV. 
Assessing the accuracy of the 10 second acceleration time is difficult due to 
simulation limitations, and linear extrapolation is an

lling by 900mV. 
Assessing the accuracy of the 10 second acceleration time is difficult due to 
simulation limitations, and linear extrapolation is an inaccurate method. However, 
Fig. 11: Voltage ramp up output at time t=0 and t=0.45s 
Fig. 12: Voltage ramp down output at time t=0 and 
t=0.45s

10 
 
these simulation results do verify that the voltage ramps up and down gradually, and 
the motor has controlled acceleration – not the case in previous circuits. 
The main limitation of this circuit design for the elevator application is that it is an 
‘open loop’. There should be closed loop feedback as described in ‘Part A, iii’. More 
people in the elevator, will affect the motor acceleration and speed with no method of 
correction. A tachometer and feedback loop should be used to constantly adjust the 
duty cycle. 
 
Part C 
Inter-departmental Collaboration 
 Mechanical Engineering: Work must be done with a mechanical team to 
ensure motor power is suitable for cabin weight / load, desired speed etc. 
Torque / speed characteristics will be important for their calculations; e.g. 
material choices must be correct for the load cases. 
 Component Procurement: Communication must be clear with a 
procurement team as circuit designs are dependent on availability / cost of 
componen

s. 
 Component Procurement: Communication must be clear with a 
procurement team as circuit designs are dependent on availability / cost of 
components. 
 Finance: A budget must be approved, so circuit design choices should be 
presented and justified to a finance department who would assess the circuits 
effectiveness in a business sense. 
 Legal / Health & Safety: Would provide guidance to ensure the designs 
meet mandatory safety requirements and legal standards. 
 
Sustainability, Ethics, and Risk Management 
Elevator systems are energy intensive and often require 24/7 operation, increasing 
energy consumption and carbon footprint. One mitigation strategy is use of 
‘regenerative braking’. Waste energy is instead captured as electricity (e.g. when a 
heavily loaded elevator travels down) and is fed back to the grid – minimising net 
energy consumption.[12] Elevator components, both mechanical and electrical, should 
be designed for a long life-cycle to reduce waste. They should

energy consumption.[12] Elevator components, both mechanical and electrical, should 
be designed for a long life-cycle to reduce waste. They should be recyclable where 
possible and must comply with the UK’s Waste Electrical and Electronic Equipment, 
‘WEEE 2013’ regulations.[13] 
The core ethical consideration is passenger safety. Circuit failure such as H-bridge 
shoot through shorting the circuit could stop the motor suddenly. Similarly, Op-amp 
failure shorting to ground would cause the voltage ramp circuit to be bypassed, and 
the motor would instantly accelerate to full speed. Any component or circuit failure 
must be overridden by an independent safety system e.g. an ‘overspeed 
governor’.[14] The whole system must comply with EN81-20 and EN81-50 safety 
standards.[15] 
Non-Technical Presentation

11 
 
 
 
 
The flowchart, ‘Fig. 13’ presents a simple overview of elevator operation. The 
‘decision’ blocks represent the necessary safety checks: 
- The motor must not receive power until the doors are closed. 
- The doors must not open until the motor has fully stopped; the tachometer 
reads zero.  
A third decision block was added to ensure the elevator doesn’t overshoot its 
destination. By checking the distance from the destination, it can decide when to 
begin the deceleration voltage ramp and stop in the correct location. 
 
Part D 
The provided circuit with a PIR motion detector has been modified to reduce energy 
usage through implementation of an LDR, seen in ‘Fig. 14 – 16’. A variable resistor 
represents the LDR, where 100% corresponds to ‘maximum darkness’. 
The circuit is now composed of two sections: 
Fig. 13: Non-technical flow chart of elevator operation

12 
 
- The LDR circuit: a voltage divider - with R5 and R4 (LDR) - that uses the 
NPN transistor Q3 as a switch. Only on when dark. 
- The PIR activated timer circuit: turns the light on when a person is detected 
(switch S1 is closed). An RC timer keeps it on for a short period of time. 
The logic of the system requires the transistor Q3 and the switch S1 to act as an 
‘AND’ gate, ensuring both must be true for the light to turn on. They are connected in 
series: if either is off the whole circuit breaks. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
‘Fig. 14’ simulates the circuit in daytime, light outside. The resistance R4 is low, so 
the voltage at the base of Q3 is too low to activate it (below 0.7V). Even with the PIR 
closed the light remains off. 
 
 
Fig. 14: LDR modified light control circuit under light conditions 
Fig. 15: LDR modified light control circuit under dark 
conditions and PIR open 
Fig. 16: LDR modified light control circuit under dark 
conditions and PIR closed

13 
 
 
‘Fig. 15 and 16’ show the two different cases of the circuit in darkness. The 
resistance R4 is high, pulling Q3 base voltage high and activating it. Only with the 
PIR closed as well does the light turn on, fulfilling the AND gate condition. The 
addition of the voltage divider does not affect the timer circuit; the light then turns off 
approximately 10 seconds after the PIR opens. 
It is important to note: the LDR must be mounted externally - outside. Otherwise, an 
‘unstable feedback loop’ would be created. It is dark and a person enters: the AND 
gate is true, and the light turns on. However, the LDR would now detect the light. The 
AND gate breaks and the light turns off. This creates a constant cycle causing the 
light to flash on and off. The LDR must detect the ambient, outside, light only.[16] 
Hardware Prototype: 
 
 
‘Fig. 17’ above is a prototype of this circuit design. It follows the exact layout of the 
Multisim circuit, though with an actual LDR instead of the v

Fig. 17’ above is a prototype of this circuit design. It follows the exact layout of the 
Multisim circuit, though with an actual LDR instead of the variable resistor. This 
prototype is evidence that the design is a practical and cost-effective solution. The 
selection of a 33kΩ resistor R5 was influenced by prototype construction - it was a 
readily available resistor value.  
Practical Limitations: 
The darkness ‘threshold’ at which the light can turn on is set by the fixed resistor R5. 
This may not be at the ideal value all year round, depending on seasons, weather 
conditions etc. Replacing R5 with a variable resistor would allow an on-site engineer 
to calibrate the system to preference. Alternatively, if a more complex circuit is 
allowed, this process could be automated. 
 
 
Fig. 17: Prototype hardware circuit

14 
 
Conclusion 
This report has worked through increasingly complex H-bridge DC motor control 
circuits, fulfilling the initial objectives. A PWM oscillator was designed to control the 
speed and acceleration of the motor. A sophisticated understanding of electronic 
components was developed in order to fully adjust the PWM duty cycle, using diodes 
and an Op-amp in original designs. Throughout the report real-world limitations were 
discovered, specifically dynamic shoot-through error in an H-bridge. If left ignored for 
practical implementation, the transistors would likely be destroyed. Multisim 
simulation helped to identify these limitations (e.g. PWM frequency variation even 
with diodes) whilst also providing its own limitations: simulation time taking far to long 
to verify circuit operation. This meant the report had to justify circuit design through 
solid presentation of theory and calculations. Part C was important to consider the 
role of circuit design in a wider compan

circuit design through 
solid presentation of theory and calculations. Part C was important to consider the 
role of circuit design in a wider company. Learning to balancing operation, cost, 
sustainability and ethics is key for real-world implementation.
